entries.title: States Want ICE Agents to Show Their Faces. The Trump Administration Is Blocking Them
entries.summary: Federal lawyers say anti-mask laws would endanger immigration agents, citing an ICE face-recognition art project that doesn’t actually work.
entries.content:
The Trump administration has sued at least five states and the city of Philadelphia over laws that supporters say would hold law enforcement—including Immigration and Customs Enforcement officers—accountable for misconduct on the job. The specifics of the laws vary, but the Trump administration takes particular issue with the fact that the laws would prohibit ICE officers from wearing masks during immigration raids, and require them to wear some sort of individual identifier.

Having to show their faces, lawyers for the Trump administration argue, would put ICE officers in danger.

In complaints filed against New York, Virginia, Connecticut, New Jersey, and Philadelphia, attorneys for the federal government claim that people take photos of ICE officers to “run them through facial recognition applications that search social media and doxing sites,” specifically calling out an art project called ICESpy. (California was subject to a similar lawsuit months before the other states, but the DOJ did not mention ICESpy in that complaint.)

There's just one problem, according to ICESpy’s creator, artist Kyle McDonald: the art project's face-recognition software cannot recognize most, if any, ICE agents currently in the field.

ICESpy is based on photos, job titles, and names of people who self-identified as ICE nearly a decade ago on LinkedIn. WIRED viewed a version of the list, which had just over 700 entries, and found that the most common title was “assistant chief counsel,” and many of the people appeared to have executive, administrative, or legal roles. Only 39 people on it had “deportation officer” in their title, and an additional 27 had related titles such as “enforcement and removal assistant” or “immigration enforcement agent.”

Many deportation officers joined the agency well after the data was collected. In January, ICE announced that it had more than doubled its number of agents and officers in just a few months, from 10,000 to 22,000. None of those additional people would be in the database, unless they had another job with ICE in 2018 (and posted about it online).

“They make this claim that people are using this site and others to search for family members. They offer no evidence of this in the filing,” McDonald tells WIRED. “The idea that someone could use a site like ICESpy and find an active ICE deportation officer in 2026 is absurd.”

ICE did not respond to a request for comment.

Data issues aside, ICESpy is not popular. WIRED reviewed activity logs for ICESpy between June 30 and July 6, which showed there were only 84 page views that week—a remarkably small number, even for a niche art project website. Only two face-match attempts were made, and the results of both were so bad that McDonald says they could not possibly be a match of anyone in his dataset of 2018 LinkedIn users.

“Like, I'm sure people were playing with it at some point?? But it is not an active threat to ANYBODY Imao,” McDonald says in a text message.

Attorneys for the federal government also blame another site, ICE List, for aiding people interested in "doxing" ICE agents. ICE List describes itself as an accountability initiative, and keeps track of people involved with ICE and other agencies involved in deportation efforts, along with vehicles used on the job and incidents that occur during enforcement actions.

Individual agent pages on ICE List typically include the person’s name and general information about what their job title is, the field office they are associated with, and any incidents they might have been involved in along with links to public sources that corroborate the claims on the page. When WIRED analyzed the site in January, we found that it too relied mostly on data that self-described ICE employees had posted on LinkedIn. It also doesn't have any sort of public-facing way for visitors to try to computationally analyze photos, though some agent pages include headshots.

Dominick Skinner, ICE List’s maintainer, says the site has a small, dedicated group of people who try to use face recognition, but their success with the technology so far has been “overblown,” and the site mostly relies on manual research.

“I wish we had a tool good enough to take away the work 😂” Skinner tells WIRED via text message.

The group of ICE List users was recently credited for identifying David Brouillette, the ICE agent and former Army veteran who allegedly shot and killed Johan Sebastián Durán Guerrero, a 25-year-old Colombian living in Maine. In that case, the website put out a request for information from people in Bangor, Maine, and Skinner says two people close to Brouillette came forward. Brouillette’s ex-wife later told the Portland Press Herald that she had previously reported concerns about Brouillette’s mental health to his superiors in the US military. Brouillette did not respond to a request for comment.

Skinner says that ICE List doesn’t fit the traditional definition of doxing—it does not post agents’ home addresses or that of their family—and that data broker sites post far more intimate information about agents but don’t face the same level of scrutiny. Older guidance from the Department of Homeland Security, which oversees ICE, advises people to request to remove their personal data from such sites.

It’s unclear how many ICE agents have actually been identified because of ICESpy or ICE List. The Department of Justice, which is litigating the cases, did not put specific numbers in its complaints.

DOJ spokesperson Kiersten Pels says that “individuals have used facial recognition and doxxing tools to identify and harass federal law enforcement officers, and these sites frequently change domains, shut down, and reappear under new names.”

Pels did not respond to WIRED’s request for a concrete example of a protester using ICESpy to identify an ICE agent.

Meanwhile, state lawmakers say their laws requiring ICE agents to identify themselves are trying to address very real issues around democracy and justice, and the federal government should prioritize other work that’s within its remit.

“Instead of challenging these reasonable restrictions, the Department of Justice should seek accountability for the actions of poorly trained ICE agents, release the Epstein files, and investigate the Trump organization’s unprecedented corruption,” says Virginia state senator Saddam Salim, where the federal government successfully won an injunction pausing SB 352, the state’s new law that prohibits law enforcement in the state from wearing masks, among other restrictions.

ICE officers weren’t wearing congressionally mandated body cameras when they fatally shot two people in Maine and Texas this month, making it more difficult to investigate what happened. Federal officials refused to produce evidence to Minnesota investigators related to the fatal shootings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti for nearly half a year until they were compelled by the courts. WIRED recently reported that ICE's Office of Professional Responsibility, an internal watchdog that investigates employee misconduct, is busy looking into civilians who post criticism online about the federal government and its immigration policies.

“States have a right and an ability to be able to react and respond to what we see as unconstitutional behavior by our federal government,” says Bob Duff, a Connecticut state senator who worked on a law for his state that is now being challenged. “And we have a right and responsibility to protect our own residents in the state.”

entries.title: A Teen Reporter Searched for His Community in the Epstein Files. Adults Freaked Out
entries.summary: An Instagram post turned one California student newspaper into a free-speech flash point. The students say they were just doing their homework.
entries.content:
Ben Mueller’s assignment for his journalism class was to tell a news story—any news story—in the form of an extended Instagram slideshow. So when the high school senior got home on the afternoon of February 10, 2026, he grabbed a snack, went upstairs to his room, and settled in to work.

After allowing himself a few minutes of procrastination—scouting prospective college campuses on Google Earth—Mueller navigated over to the Department of Justice’s Epstein Library portal and clicked through the page asking whether he was 18 or older (he was, just barely). Then he began poking around for ideas in the 3 million pages of Epstein files that had been added 11 days earlier.

Mueller really liked working in his bedroom, with its beanbag chair and view of people strolling and biking along one of Marin County, California’s sun-dappled waterways. Having lived his whole life in one of the wealthiest parts of America, Mueller couldn’t help thinking his area probably had some ties to Jeffrey Epstein. “I think it’s very difficult to get an immense amount of money without—this is my opinion—without doing something a little shady,” Mueller says. So he sat on his bedroom floor and spent the next five hours combing the federal database for links to the towns and cities of Marin.

He found thousands. Most of them were trivial (Epstein liked to shop online at Restoration Hardware, headquartered in nearby Corte Madera). But a few things stood out: a 2014 art gala in Sausalito that Epstein helped promote; a meeting with Woody Allen in Tiburon; and—above all—a 2013 email exchange between Epstein and a woman from a modeling agency called Mill Valley Connection. “Regarding Hannah, my Austrian model,” the woman wrote in one particularly chilling message, “we can come and see you this evening if you are interested, and in that case let me know how I can get my fees.”

Two days later, on February 12, Mueller posted his story to the Instagram feed of the Redwood Bark, his school’s student-run publication. It was a carousel-style slideshow pitched at readers across the county. “Which Marin towns were mentioned in the Epstein files?” the first panel reads. “Scroll to see how many times your town was mentioned.” Rather than quote from the most alarming document he’d found, Mueller kept his descriptions fairly general and brief: “Mill Valley was mentioned in the context of providing models to Epstein from a woman named Gisele Attias Bonnouvrier.”

More than 4,000 people liked the story. The comments filled up with praise: “This is the kind of journalism we need.” “Higher quality reporting than local outlets.” “High schoolers doing better reporting than national news.” “Didn’t think I’d be following a random CA HS newspaper in 2026 for their breaking reporting but here we are.”

As Mueller watched the numbers climb, he felt excited but also a little perplexed. “I didn’t really think I was doing groundbreaking journalism,” he says. “I was just like, this is another piece of homework that I have to do today, and I’ve just got to get it done—but also be thorough, because people are going to see it on the internet.”

About a week and a half later, Mueller was home sick with a light fever and cough when the phone rang. It was his journalism teacher, Erin Schneider, with bad news. “This person mentioned in your post is now threatening to sue the district,” she said.

On Monday, February 23, Bonnouvrier, the modeling agent, had sent an email to the principal of Redwood High School, calling Mueller’s post defamatory, demanding its immediate removal, and promising legal action if the school did not comply within 48 hours. And now the district superintendent was demanding that the Bark either redact Bonnouvrier’s name or delete the slide entirely.

The redwood bark happens to be one of the most award-winning high school newspapers in the United States. Madison Bishop—a poised, rosy-cheeked, 18-year old colleague of Mueller’s—says she was an avid Bark reader even as a kid, before her older sister served as editor in chief back in 2022. “It was just such a well-known student paper,” she says.

Bishop and Mueller both arrived on the Bark staff during a time of experimentation. Over the previous decade, Schneider and her students had pushed the Bark beyond old-school print and basic web articles, expanding into Instagram, Facebook, TikTok, LinkedIn, and a podcast. Then during the first few weeks of this past school year, Schneider says, they flipped the switch toward being “social-media first”—publishing some full stories directly to the platforms.

As the Bark’s social media manager, Bishop oversaw the transition, which she saw as a kind of power move: Social media wasn’t just where all the Bark’s students got their news, it’s where kids have the most natural authority. “It gives us a space to have a leg up from adults,” she says. “It really lets them sit down and listen and absorb what we have to say, because we are the quote-unquote professionals.”

Case in point: As soon as the Bark leaders saw the superintendent’s demands, they knew Instagram wouldn’t allow them to edit an already published slide; simply redacting Bonnouvrier’s name, in other words, was not a viable option. Another problem was that Redwood High’s principal was out of the country and unable to meet. With the pressure of a 48-hour legal threat looming over them, they would have to decide how to respond to the district on their own.

Once everyone was back at school on Tuesday, the high schoolers lawyered up during their lunch hour. Gathered around the speakerphone in Schneider’s office, Bishop and Mueller consulted an attorney with the national Student Press Law Center. When he heard the students’ problem, the lawyer laughed, Schneider recalls. “If this woman thinks she’s going to sue your school district, she’s got another thing coming,” he said. “Her name is in the Epstein files—no lawyer is going to represent her in this case.” The students also consulted Mueller’s parents—both lawyers—and another attorney, who all concluded that the Bark had done nothing wrong.

Increasingly convinced that they hadn’t defamed anyone, the Bark staffers weighed their options. After school, Mueller, Schneider, Bishop, and another Bark editor sat down on the blue couches in their newsroom for a grueling four-hour debate. Painstakingly, they double-checked every single word on Mueller’s slide, verifying it against the federal documents.

They were on the verge of deciding to leave the Instagram post alone. But after Schneider headed out for the evening, the students kept second-guessing themselves. They ended up deciding to temporarily archive Mueller’s most controversial slide until they could meet with school officials in person. “I think at the end of the day, the kids felt such pressure from the administration that they were almost looking for a reason to get out from under it,” says Schneider, who had butted heads with school officials several times that year over the Bark. (Schneider is also someone I’ve known for years; I’m a massage therapist in Marin County, and Schneider used to be an occasional client before I started working as a journalist.)

That evening, the students wrote a note advising Schneider what they wanted to communicate to the principal; they framed their takedown of Mueller’s slide as entirely their own prerogative. “We want to be clear that the removal was not in response to your request,” they wrote. “Our choice was based on our own ethical considerations and editorial standards.”

The message was puzzling to Schneider, who saw the administrators’ demand as a pretty clear example of censorship. But the Bark reporters didn’t see themselves as having given in.

Finally, that Thursday, Mueller and Bishop met in person with principal Barnaby Payne and Courtney Goode, superintendent of the Tamalpais Union High School District. Schneider came along too, but only to take notes. As she remembers it, the meeting started tense.

Goode opened by explaining that the district couldn’t possibly afford a legal battle. Mueller and Bishop stayed quiet, letting the administrators finish before asking them to clarify exactly what was libelous about the post.

The administrators, it turned out, were primarily worried that Mueller had reported something that simply wasn’t true. The district’s own research, Goode explained, had found no evidence that Bonnouvrier was connected to providing models to Epstein. “You need to link your source to this,” Goode said.

Realizing the adults had likely just done a basic Google search, which couldn’t access files deep in the federal Epstein database, Mueller pulled out his phone and opened to the specific document, showing his screen. “The source is just the Epstein files,” Mueller said. “It is true, because it says it right here in a government document.” On the screen was Bonnouvrier’s email about her “Austrian model,” which seemed to take the two men aback.

The high schoolers then launched into a breakdown of California Education Code 48907, reminding the administrators of state law. Unless content is obscene, incites violence or illegal activity, or is false or libelous, student journalists have total freedom of expression without administrative oversight.

“Well, you know, I just had to make sure that we weren’t going to get sued,” Goode said, according to Schneider’s notes.

“With all due respect,” Schneider remembers Bishop replying, “under California’s student press law, being afraid of a lawsuit or backlash is not a valid reason to call for the takedown of content.”

Bishop described the 30-minute meeting as “a very open conversation” that ended with everyone “on the same page that the post legally does not have to be taken down.” (In a statement to WIRED, Goode said the post was “briefly paused” until “it was determined that the information referenced was factual and publicly available.”) The next day, Mueller and Bishop went back to the newsroom and restored the archived Instagram slide.

Bonnouvrier, who declined to comment on this story, never made good on her legal threat. But the fallout was far from over. About two weeks after the meeting, Schneider sent an email to all Bark students and their parents. “I am writing to let you know that I will be stepping away on a leave from the journalism program effective today, Monday, March 9,” she wrote.

For Schneider, the clash over Mueller’s post had been one of the final straws in what she regarded as a yearlong battle over administrative interference. In another recent incident, the Bark had published a cover image of a student protest against the Trump administration held in San Francisco. The demonstrators in the photo are holding up a banner that says “Students Fight Back.” In smaller print, the banner includes the words “Against Zionism.” After reader objections rolled in, including one Instagram comment comparing the Bark to the Ku Klux Klan, the school district announced it was opening a mandatory investigation; board policy required as much, they said, in any case of public complaints.

“The work of defending First Amendment rights and helping young journalists report responsibly on real-world topics is work I believe in deeply,” Schneider wrote in her farewell email. “It is also work that has met significant resistance.” Her departure caught the Bark staff completely by surprise. In a newsroom meeting that morning, the paper’s student leaders broke the news to their colleagues through tears.

It wasn’t until more than a month later, after emotions had cooled and the Redwood students had returned from spring break, that the story of Mueller’s post blew up beyond Marin. “Small High School Sparks Fury Over Witch Hunt Into Student Paper for Exposing Woman’s Link to Epstein,” ran a headline in the News Corp.–owned California Post. “High school administrators censored a student newspaper,” began a post by the Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression, a campus free-speech watchdog, which also posted a video about the incident to social media. Stories also ran on California Public Radio and the news site EdSource.

To some commenters, the Redwood High dispute was a case of liberal elite corruption in Governor Gavin Newsom’s California. (Newsom is a Redwood alum.) To others it mirrored a broader national climate of intimidation and administrative crackdowns on every level of American life. Indeed, the Student Press Law Center says it has experienced a 42 percent increase in legal defense requests from school newspapers to its legal hotline over the past two years—as students use confidential sources to report on hot-button topics (immigration raids, Gaza, campus protests, the Epstein files) and administrators try to keep a low profile by clamping down.

The Redwood High students found most of the coverage annoying. Mueller and the other Bark staffers felt increasingly uncomfortable being cast as victims. “Calling it censorship really convolutes the story,” says Mueller, who didn’t like being painted as “weak and submissive” and scared of a principal. In an editorial note posted on May 27, the Bark staff stressed that they had briefly removed their slide by choice, calling the standoff with administrators a “simple misunderstanding” rather than a high-handed abuse of power.

But mostly—with AP classes and Bark assignments and graduation looming—Mueller just wanted to put the story behind him. “It was a little tiring just to see it come back up,” he says. “People are making a big deal about this because it feels like something more serious than it is.”

As it happened, Bishop had spent much of the school year reporting on student journalism controversies. She’d made a short documentary on the censorship-driven collapse of nearby Novato High’s newspaper, covered the forced reassignment of a journalism adviser at Lowell High School in San Francisco, and reported on a Virginia student newsroom that successfully resisted administrative control—and for all that work, Bishop had earned third place in a competition for California Student Journalist of the Year. But she didn’t really see the Bark controversy in the same light. The students had kept hold of the reins, in her mind, through one adult intervention after another. “At the end of the day, all decisions are final by the students,” she said this spring. “I know sometimes it can get distracting with external, adult conflicts. They disagree. They want this taken down. They’re leaving. But it is a student-run class, and students are the voice of the paper.”

In June, Mueller and Bishop themselves edged closer to the world of adults who have no business telling Bark staffers what to report. On the 11th, they both graduated. Bishop is heading to the University of Southern California to study journalism. Mueller is set to attend William and Mary in Virginia. He’s keeping his options open but says he might check out the college paper.

entries.title: Flipper Busy Bar Review: An Expensive Focus Tool
entries.summary: It didn’t keep my coworkers at bay, but Flipper’s expensive productivity tool did help me focus on the task at hand.
entries.content:
The Busy Bar is a focus tool that blares a bright red BUSY sign. Stick it on your desk, mount it on your monitor, or put it outside your office—the device works as both a productivity timer and an indication that you are not to be interrupted. It costs $250.

It's made by Flipper Devices, the company known for the Flipper Zero—a handheld hacking tool that uses a Tamagotchi-esque Dolphin to monitor and manipulate certain radio-frequency identification (RFID) frequencies. It freaked some governments out enough that they temporarily banned the product. But the Busy Bar is a much quieter device, meant to sit on your desk or hang on your monitor to let people know you’re in the zone and not to be disturbed. It was announced in 2025, and sales kicked off in June. Flipper recently released the open source firmware on GitHub.

The device is small, roughly the size of a sunglasses case. The default screen reads BUSY, but you can swap between themes that change the message on the screen, like “KEEP OUT,” “OUT TO LUNCH,” or “LOW SOCIAL BATTERY.” The bar also works as a digital clock, and for now that’s the only other app on it.

entries.title: Is the All-New Range Rover GT Stepping on Jaguar’s Tail?
entries.summary: It’s “the most car-like Range Rover ever created,” but will this all-electric grand tourer spoil Jaguar’s Type 01 party?
entries.content:
In what looks to be a radical departure from its existing lineup, Range Rover has confirmed an entirely new fifth vehicle: the Range Rover GT, an all-electric grand tourer built on the company's Electrified Modular Architecture (EMA), the same platform that will underpin other midsized cars for the brand.

Range Rover has only shared camouflaged prototype pictures for now, as the GT is supposedly still undergoing final testing, ahead of a full launch expected later this year. That means details on this completely new model are scant to say the least: no price, no range, and no power figures have been disclosed. As for a release date, Range Rover confirmed the GT hits the road in 2027.

What is evident from the camo images is the GT's sleek silhouette and coupé proportions, more typically associated with Range Rover's sister brand, Jaguar, than with the marque known for its commitment to SUVs. Both companies sit below the parent company, JLR (formerly spelled out as Jaguar Land Rover).

In a statement, Martin Limpert, global managing director at Range Rover, says the Range Rover GT is intended to “redefine the grand-touring segment” with its “GT poise, proportions and long-distance refinement,” adding that the EV will offer “capabilities no conventional GT can match.”

The inclusion of the word “conventional” is doing some heavy lifting. Range Rover will likely take pains in the coming months explaining how this new all-electric grand tourer isn't a direct competitor to Jaguar's new all-electric grand tourer, the hugely anticipated and polarizing Type 01, intended to signify a complete reset for Jaguar, also arriving in 2027.

“We have spent the last few years working obsessively on the fundamentals of the GT formula, reinterpreted in a sophisticated, uniquely Range Rover way,” says Limpert, adding that this GT, like its SUV stablemates, will apparently still be able to off-road. “The result is the most car-like Range Rover ever created. Its blend of effortless EV performance, first-class long-haul refinement, and all-terrain capability is pure Range Rover, while its interior previews our vision of a modern grand tourer.”

The images of the Range Rover GT interior show a clean, minimalist cabin with plenty of legroom for rear passengers, a panoramic roof, and in the front a wide, slender driver display coupled with a larger central screen. A concealed air vent runs the width of the cabin, while speakers are hidden beneath the same textile that wraps the dashboard. However, Range Rover has stuck with touchscreens for all controls, going against the auto industry trend, with what looks like no dedicated physical switchgear, even for climate control.

Directly comparing the GT's exterior and interior design with that of the coming Jaguar Type 01, it's clear there are family resemblances.

The GT will be built on JLR's EMA architecture at the company's Halewood plant in the UK, the same midsize platform that will power the next-gen replacements for the Evoque EV, the Land Rover Discovery Sport, an electric Range Rover Velar successor, and the incoming “Baby Defender.”

However, EMA is not the architecture that will be used for the incoming, larger Range Rover Electric—which will run on JLR's Modular Longitudinal Architecture (MLA) and be built in Solihull in the UK. Crucially, it's this larger MLA platform that is the base for the Jaguar Type 01. Range Rover says the GT will arrive in EV form initially, but be followed by hybrid options “later in the lifecycle” of the car. Jaguar is committed to being an electric-only brand, despite the MLA being capable of supporting mild-hybrid and full-hybrid variants.

So, are Range Rover GT and Jaguar Type 01 competing for the same customer? It's a question I put to Range Rover, and got this reply from a spokesperson: “Range Rover and Jaguar are two very different brands, and the vehicles sold under each are very different propositions. Jaguar Type 01 is an electric four-door luxury GT, while Range Rover GT is a new take on the Range Rover formula, the first vehicle to sit on the EMA platform, retaining Range Rover’s all-terrain capability.”

It's not surprising that Range Rover insists there is a significant difference between its new model and Jaguar's EV relaunch. However, it will have to reveal more on the GT to end such obvious comparisons. After all, there's no getting away from the fact that both the Range Rover and Jaguar cars are grand tourers, both launching next year, both from the same parent company, both electric, both chasing wealthy buyers who want comfort and refinement for long-distance driving over outright off-road ability.

Despite having different architectures underneath, if the highest-spec Range Rover GT ends up being priced anywhere near (or, perhaps worse, undercuts) Jaguar Type 01 territory, JLR will be, somewhat bafflingly, fielding two four-door luxury GT EVs from two of its own brands into a very similar buyer pool.

entries.title: OpenAI Models Escaped Containment and Hacked Hugging Face
entries.summary: The cybersecurity-focused models, including GPT-5.6 Sol, broke out of a testing sandbox, exploited a zero-day, and gained access to the open internet to pull off the attack.
entries.content:
OpenAI disclosed on Tuesday that it lost control of two AI models during a security test that ended in a breach of the AI research platform Hugging Face.

Describing the incident as “unprecedented,” OpenAI said its AI models broke out of a sealed testing environment last week and hacked into Hugging Face’s production system to steal the answers to a test they were being graded on. The models—the publicly available GPT-5.6 Sol and an unreleased, reportedly more capable one—were being evaluated on their offensive hacking skills with the safeguards that normally block high-risk cyber activity switched off.

“The models identified and chained vulnerabilities across OpenAI’s research environment and Hugging Face’s production infrastructure to obtain test solutions directly from Hugging Face’s production database,” OpenAI and Hugging Face wrote in a joint blog post disclosing the intrusion.

According to OpenAI and Hugging Face, the models escaped through a package registry cache proxy—software that allows developers to install outside code without connecting to the internet. The proxy was the only component in OpenAI’s isolated testing environment permitted to reach the outside world; in normal use that reach extends only to public code repositories.

Rather than stay contained in the sandbox, the models ​​“exploited a zero-day vulnerability” to gain access to the open internet as they “hyperfocused” on finding a solution for the AI cybersecurity benchmark known as ExploitGym. Such experiments involve prompting that pressures the models to find solutions, essentially egging them on.

“After gaining internet access, the models inferred that Hugging Face potentially hosted models, datasets and solutions for ExploitGym,” OpenAI wrote. “Knowing this, the model searched for and successfully found ways to gain access to secret information that it could use to cheat the evaluation. In one example, the model chained together multiple attack vectors, including using stolen credentials and zero-day.”

The flaw the models exploited was previously unknown, but flaws in this kind of software are not unusual. Companies have been patching serious vulnerabilities in artifact repositories for a decade. A bug disclosed in 2024 let anyone who could reach the server ask for a file by URL and get it—configurations files, passwords, access tokens—without logging in. Others have let attackers take control of the server itself.

Researchers point out that while AI advances have created new and sometimes unexpected challenges, the task of extensively and rigorously isolating infrastructure from the open internet is well explored.

“This is not an AI problem. It’s negligence on a 40-year-old standard—and it’s basically every sci-fi film ever,” says longtime security and compliance consultant Davi Ottenheimer. “‘Highly isolated’ and ‘escaped through the one hole we left open’ cannot both be true.”

In recent months, top AI companies have been raising concerns about the expanding cybersecurity capabilities of upcoming frontier models as the platforms increase in both expertise, creativity, and agentic, autonomous operation. But researchers emphasize that this is all the more reason that fundamentals should still apply.

“This should not have happened,” says veteran security engineer and researcher Niels Provos. “I wish the frontier labs spent as much time on teaching their models to write secure infrastructure as they are spending on them exploiting vulnerabilities.”

entries.title: Shop Best Buy’s Black Friday in July Sale 2026 Before Fall Chaos
entries.summary: The discounts may not beat Prime Day, but I found worthwhile prices on WIRED-approved TVs, laptops, tablets, speakers, and other home gear.
entries.content:
TIRED of the heat waves and wildfire smoke? Just pretend it’s November already with Best Buy’s annual Black Friday in July sale, happening now. The deals aren’t necessarily as good as they were on Prime Day, but if you missed out on that event, this is your next best bet to grab that laptop or tablet for less. It’s a good buying opportunity if your kid needs new gear for their upcoming semester at college or if you’d simply rather avoid the fall rush to shop for a new TV.

We test all the latest devices year-round and give our honest takes on everything. Below are hand-picked deals on gear our experts would buy for themselves. Don't wait—the deals end Sunday, July 26.

For more in-depth information on our testing, check out our guides to the Best TVs, Best Art TVs, Best Laptops, Best iPad to Buy, Best Tablets, Best Headphones, and Best Bluetooth Speakers.

TV Deals

LG

B5 OLED

We crowned this model the best budget OLED TV, and it’s one of the best value buys available, even when it’s not on sale. We especially like its natural-looking colors and solid 4K gaming bona fides.

Hisense

CanvasTV S7N

Art TVs are hot right now, and this is one of our favorites. When you’re not using it as a TV, it comes with a library of 1,000 non-subscription works of art you can display. Or you can just feature your own photos from your Google account.

Apple Deals

Apple

MacBook Air (M5, 2026)

The best MacBook for most people, this model features Apple’s most recent processor, the M5 chip. Given that Apple recently raised its price by $200 on the 13-inch model, this is $100 more than the base price you may have seen earlier this year, but it's as good a deal as you'll find right now.

Apple

AirPods Pro 3

Apple's latest AirPods Pro are the most popular headphones on the market. They earned a rare 9/10 rating from former WIRED A/V editor Parker Hall for their improved noise-cancelling capability, selection of four ear tip sizes, and new tools, like heart-rate sensing and live translation.

Tablet Deals

Samsung

Galaxy Tab S10+ and Tab S10 Ultra

Save $350 on the Ultra model of this previous-generation tablet that’s easy to love, with multiday battery life and a smooth display.

Apple

iPad (2025, A16)

The iPad we recommended for most people is $50 off right now and sports Apple’s new A16 chip. There’s not enough RAM to support Apple Intelligence, but this is still a well-rounded-enough tablet to consider.

Bluetooth Speaker Deals

JBL

Flip 7

Save $50 on one of our favorite Bluetooth speakers that can take all manner of abuse. It's IP68 water-resistant, shock-tested for 1-meter drops, and has a 14-hour battery life.

Bose

Soundlink Plus

Most outlets are knocking $70 off the price of our favorite midsize speaker right now, but Best Buy is the seller that carries all the colors, including this cool Citrus Yellow, Sunset Peach, and Blue Dusk.

Home Tech Deals

Roborock

Qrevo Curv

This 2024 flagship model is not new, but former WIRED reviewer Ryan Waniata said it was the best robot vacuum he’d ever used. This is one of the best prices for it that you’ll see.

Ninja

Slushi Machine

Fifty bucks off isn’t exactly a doorbuster for this machine, but come on, who doesn’t need a frozen marg? This was our favorite slushie machine until we tried the Ninja XL, but it’s still a great entry-level model if you're looking to round out the rest of your summer with frozen drinks.
